coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind the 0.80 priority-floor policy assumption first.
coaching.subject: the 0.80 priority-floor policy assumption

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `verify.enforce_execution_priority_floor` flag (defaulting to false, enabled for rebar) to gate the 0.80 blocking-threshold change behind the same staged-rollout pattern used for the other two opt-in gates, or explicitly document a tested, cheap undo path faster than full code redeploy.
evidence: 'During execution, normal Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution applies effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) to blocking criteria.'
'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
'A runtime floor flag is intentionally not added.'
Both other behavioral gates (enforce_plan_material_pins, require_plan_review_for_close) are explicitly flag-gated and default to false; this one is not.
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-review blocking-threshold floor (`effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)`) takes effect for all execution-phase reviews immediately on deployment with no feature flag. The plan explicitly refuses a runtime flag ('A runtime floor flag is intentionally not added'), so there is no way to canary or gradually roll out this behavioral change short of a full code redeploy.
findings.location: Settled design › Execution reviews and close gate — 'During execution, normal Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution applies effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)' and 'A runtime floor flag is intentionally not added.'
scenarios: Any project with tickets currently in_progress immediately receives the raised blocking floor upon upgrade, potentially surfacing new blocking findings in reviews that previously passed — a one-shot 100%-traffic flip for execution-phase reviews with no canary option.

If the floor causes unexpected blocking findings in production work, operators must perform a full code redeploy to revert, which is more expensive and slower than flipping a config flag; this conflicts with the T12 'explicit, cheap, tested way to undo quickly' requirement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `verify.enforce_execution_priority_floor: bool = False` alongside the two existing enforcement flags, enable it in the rebar project config, and update the rollback narrative to name flag-disable as the first-response undo path for this behavior. The AC line 'Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 priority-floor' remains satisfied because rebar enables the flag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the tail-item fixture corpus for packed prerequisite evaluation: state the minimum fixture count, how ground-truth consistency labels are established (one-per-call oracle or human annotation), and the acceptance threshold (e.g., 'across N fixtures, packed recall ≥ one-per-call recall, false-acceptance rate ≤ one-per-call rate, all prerequisites covered, zero attribution errors').
evidence: 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline: packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero. Tail-item fixtures are mandatory.'

ABSENCE: no specification of fixture count, source, diversity requirements, or how 'recall' and 'false acceptance' are operationally computed (e.g., human-labeled ground truth vs. one-per-call results treated as oracle)
findings.finding: The AC 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract' depends on recall and false-acceptance measurements that require a defined test corpus, but the plan never specifies the test fixtures' size, sourcing, or adequacy criteria — only that 'tail-item fixtures are mandatory'. Without a specified corpus, an executor cannot determine in-session whether the fidelity contract has been met; two agents could produce different fixture sets and reach opposite pass/fail conclusions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract'; Settled design — 'Prerequisite consistency review' paragraph on packed behavior
scenarios: An executor creates two tail-item fixtures and claims passing fidelity; a reviewer expects a larger diverse corpus and rejects.

Recall cannot be computed without a ground-truth label for each prerequisite-consistency judgment, but the plan does not specify whether one-per-call outputs serve as the oracle or human labels are required.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under 'Prerequisite consistency review' that states: (1) the gold baseline is constructed by running one-per-call evaluation on a fixed fixture set of at least N ticket pairs (suggest N ≥ 20, including at least two tail-item cases where total prerequisite text approaches the context limit); (2) one-per-call outputs on those fixtures are the oracle; (3) packed evaluation passes when its outputs match on every fixture by the four stated metrics. Reference these fixtures as checked-in test artifacts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'force transitions' override or are overridden by the 'open always resets' rule — explicitly state that force-to-open does (or does not) reset plan_review_phase, and remove the ambiguity introduced by 'including force transitions'.
evidence: 'a winning committed STATUS event targeting in_progress sets execution, one targeting open sets planning, and other targets preserve the value, including force transitions'
'A later open event always resets it'
The phrase 'including force transitions' is syntactically attached to 'other targets preserve the value', but a force transition targeting open is also an 'open event' per the second clause.
findings.finding: The phase-update rule for force transitions contradicts the open-resets rule. The body states 'other targets preserve the value, including force transitions' (implying force-to-open preserves), but also states 'A later open event always resets it' (implying force-to-open resets). An implementer targeting force-close-to-open faces two incompatible instructions.
findings.location: Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate — phase update rule paragraph
scenarios: An agent force-transitions a ticket from blocked back to open; one clause says phase is preserved (still execution), the other says it resets to planning — different consumers would behave differently depending on which clause they implement.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the sentence to: 'other targets preserve the value; force transitions to non-open, non-in_progress targets also preserve the value.' Then let 'A later open event always resets it' apply uniformly, including to force-to-open.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC covering rollback safety: e.g., 'After disabling both enforcement flags and deploying the preceding code version, no stored attestation requires rewriting and plan_review_phase-bearing snapshots are reduced correctly by the older reducer.'
evidence: 'Rollback has two explicit paths. To disable only the opt-in gates, set enforce_plan_material_pins=false and require_plan_review_for_close=false… To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior, first disable both gates, then deploy the preceding code version; a runtime floor flag is intentionally not added. No stored attestation must be rewritten.'
No AC in the Acceptance Criteria section addresses rollback correctness, attestation immutability during rollback, or that no stored attestation is rewritten.
findings.finding: Two explicit rollback paths are fully described in the body ('To disable only the opt-in gates…' and 'To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior…') but no acceptance criterion verifies rollback safety or correctness — leaving this described deliverable uncovered.
findings.location: Settled design → Enforcement and rollout — rollback paragraph
scenarios: A reviewer approving the implementation has no AC to verify that the two rollback paths actually leave stored attestations intact or that the enforcement flags behave correctly during a rollback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC 4 to reflect the retry-then-unsigned semantics, e.g.: 'Pre-sign re-collection retries up to three times on any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change; exhaustion returns a retryable unsigned result rather than a signed attestation.'
evidence: AC: 'Pre-sign re-collection aborts after any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change'

Body: 'retry unless equal. Acquire the global lock and require HEAD still equals revision-after… On revision change retry outside-lock collection at most three times; exhaustion returns retryable unsigned. LockTimeout during the pre-sign commit has the same retryable unsigned disposition.'
'Aborts' implies single-shot failure; the body specifies a bounded retry loop before returning unsigned.
findings.finding: AC 4 says 'Pre-sign re-collection aborts after any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change', but the body specifies retry-up-to-three-times behavior followed by 'retryable unsigned' return — not a single-attempt abort. This terminology mismatch could cause an implementer to omit the retry loop or treat the first-change detection as a permanent failure.
findings.location: AC line: 'Pre-sign re-collection aborts after any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change' vs. Settled design → Signed plan-material pins — pre-sign transaction paragraph
scenarios: An implementer reading only the AC implements a single-attempt abort with no retry, diverging from the body's three-retry protocol and making the operation unnecessarily fragile under light concurrent load.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC 4 with: 'Pre-sign re-collection detects any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change, retries collection up to three times, and returns a retryable unsigned result on exhaustion.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the backward-compatibility guarantee to explicitly state that review-phase and priority-floor manifest lines (not only pin lines) are also safely ignored by pre-feature verifiers and parsers, and confirm the rollback semantic (execution attestations become legacy-planning-equivalent under old code).
evidence: "an older verifier authenticates that stored list including unknown pin lines, then its field-specific parsers ignore lines they do not consume" — only pin lines are named

"To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior, first disable both gates, then deploy the preceding code version" — does not state what the old code does when it encounters review-phase/priority-floor lines in already-signed attestations
findings.finding: The backward-compatibility guarantee explicitly covers only 'unknown pin lines' being ignored by older verifiers, but the equally new review-phase and priority-floor manifest lines are not explicitly included in that guarantee. During rollback to pre-feature code, the plan does not confirm that attestations containing review-phase/priority-floor lines are safely treated as legacy planning reviews by the old parser, leaving a gap in the rollback story for execution-phase attestations.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins (backward verification paragraph); Settled design > Enforcement and rollout (rollback paths paragraph)
scenarios: Team rolls back to pre-feature code after some tickets have been signed with execution-phase reviews; old code evaluates those attestations and the plan does not confirm whether review-phase/priority-floor lines are silently ignored or cause unexpected parser behavior.
Audit tooling predating this feature encounters a priority-floor line and the plan provides no explicit compatibility statement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the backward-verification paragraph: 'The same field-specific-parser isolation applies to the review-phase and priority-floor lines: pre-feature readers have no parser for these fields, so they are silently skipped and the attestation is evaluated as a legacy planning review.' Also add a note to the rollback section confirming this semantic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign explicit documentation homes (or confirm they share the same 'advanced attestation/gate documentation' location) for verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, verify.require_plan_review_for_close, the DerivedPlanReviewHealth/TargetPinDetail contract, and the review-phase/priority-floor manifest fields.
evidence: "plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation" — only pin syntax is called out

ABSENCE: no documentation artifact named for enforce_plan_material_pins, require_plan_review_for_close, DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema, TargetPinDetail schema, or review-phase/priority-floor manifest fields
findings.finding: The plan names 'advanced attestation/gate documentation' as the documentation home only for plan-material-pin manifest syntax. The two new operator-facing config flags (verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, verify.require_plan_review_for_close), the new DerivedPlanReviewHealth/TargetPinDetail API contract shape, and the expanded manifest field list (review-phase, priority-floor) are not assigned to any documentation artifact or ADR.
findings.location: Settled design > Health and documentation; Settled design > Enforcement and rollout
scenarios: An operator wants to enable enforcement and cannot find the flag name or its semantics in any doc.
A consumer integrating MCP ticket-detail output needs the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema and has no doc reference.
An existing manifest format reference doc becomes stale because review-phase and priority-floor lines are unmentioned.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the 'Health and documentation' section with: 'The advanced attestation/gate documentation is updated to cover: the enforce_plan_material_pins and require_plan_review_for_close config flags and their defaults; the DerivedPlanReviewHealth and TargetPinDetail schema; the review-phase and priority-floor manifest fields and their syntax rules.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation task to this plan (or a linked child ticket) for an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing: the signed-pin manifest extension rationale, the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract stability expectation, the plan_review_phase projection lifecycle rules, and the two explicit rollback paths.
evidence: No mention of an ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design document in any section of the plan.
'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation' — this names user-facing docs but not architectural decision records.
DerivedPlanReviewHealth, TargetPinDetail, PrerequisiteCoverageRecord, and manifest ordering are new contracts affecting claim, close, audit, MCP, CLI, and web UI consumers.
The rollback section describes two explicit paths but this decision rationale is not directed to any durable artifact.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions — signed plan-material pins extending the attestation model, the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract and its exact enum values, the manifest field ordering and pin-line syntax, the PrerequisiteCoverageRecord schema, and the plan_review_phase projection lifecycle — but names no ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update to capture them.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins; Settled design > Enforcement and rollout; Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review; Health and documentation
scenarios: A new contributor modifying any of the six named consumers has no single document explaining why pin lines are additive, why phase-less manifests default to planning, or why enforcement defaults to false — they must reconstruct intent from code comments.
A future plan-review epic author does not know whether DerivedPlanReviewHealth is a stable contract or an internal detail, leading to either accidental breakage or unnecessary coupling.
The two explicit rollback paths (disable flags vs. revert binary) are described only in the plan ticket, which may be archived; future operators have no canonical reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the title or Problem/Outcome section that the epic also introduces active prerequisite consistency evaluation (not just fingerprint-based currency tracking), or explicitly justify why it is integral rather than a separable concern.
evidence: Title: 'Keep plan-review attestations current across relationships and execution'
Problem: 'an agent can therefore claim or close work using assumptions that no longer match the plans it relies on'

Settled design: 'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist... Pass 1 returns exactly one PrerequisiteCoverageRecord per canonical prerequisite. Its schema is {prerequisite_id, disposition: consistent|finding|indeterminate, findings: list[Finding]...}'
The consistency review can produce new LLM findings and block certification, which exceeds the 'keep current' framing.
findings.finding: The prerequisite LLM consistency review (a full new Pass 1/2/3 evaluation pipeline with PrerequisiteCoverageRecord, batching, model-ladder escalation, attribution verification) goes meaningfully beyond 'keeping attestations current' — it adds active semantic consistency evaluation as a first-class review criterion. The title and Problem statement frame the work as freshness/currency tracking, but this component introduces new LLM-evaluated findings that can influence review outcomes (e.g., yielding INDETERMINATE reviews), which is a scope expansion not signalled by the title.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review; Title; Problem
scenarios: An implementer reading the title budgets for a fingerprint-pinning task but encounters a full new LLM-based review criterion requiring Pass 1/2/3 pipeline changes, attribution verification, and batching — significant underestimated scope.
A reviewer assessing the plan for scope creep would not anticipate the prerequisite consistency evaluation from the title alone.
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the Outcome to read: 'Every new plan review records signed plan-material pins for related tickets AND evaluates semantic consistency with direct prerequisites, so drift and inconsistency are both detected at review time.' Alternatively, break the LLM consistency criterion into a separate epic and reference it here as a dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC that defines a scenario-level or end-to-end validation mechanism (e.g., 'Given ticket B depends on ticket A and A's plan material changes after B's review, then B's attestation health shows stale-pin-drift and close enforcement blocks B's close when both flags are enabled') to demonstrate the attestation-currency goal is observably achieved.
evidence: All ACs are of the form 'Every new passing review signs...', 'Legacy unpinned attestations remain valid', 'Enabled close enforcement requires...' — purely behavioral system assertions.
No AC describes a scenario such as 'Given a prerequisite plan changes, the downstream review is flagged as stale and an agent is blocked from closing the dependent ticket.'
F4(d) requires at least one criterion with a concrete validation mechanism (before/after workflow comparison, operational metric target, dogfooding).
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion provides a concrete end-to-end validation mechanism (before/after workflow scenario, operational metric, or dogfooding test) for the overall feature. All 22 ACs are system-behavioral assertions about internal state; none describes a scenario-level or user-observable validation that the attestation-currency goal is actually achieved in practice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 22 items)
scenarios: After implementation, it is impossible to tell from the ACs alone whether the feature actually prevents agents from acting on stale assumptions in practice — only that internal state transitions are correct.
QA or acceptance testing has no scenario-level test to run; every check is a unit-behavioral assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A simulated end-to-end scenario — prerequisite plan changes post-review, downstream attestation health transitions to stale-pin-drift, and an enforcement-enabled close of the downstream ticket is blocked with a directive to re-review — passes in the rebar project integration suite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that the 'priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError' constraint is the SOLE responsibility of `compute_plan_review_validity` (the combined call site that observes all three fields together), and that individual parsers `manifest_review_phase()`, `manifest_priority_floor()`, and `manifest_pins()` must NOT raise ManifestFormatError for this cross-field condition — they return their per-line defaults in isolation, and the cross-field check is injected only at the combined validity-check site.
evidence: Plan text: 'manifest_review_phase() returns planning only when the phase line is absent' — this is the legacy sentinel, indistinguishable from a malformed manifest that has a floor line but no phase line without inspecting both.
Plan text: 'a priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError' — this is a cross-field constraint requiring joint inspection of two manifest lines.

Plan text: 'compute_plan_review_validity parses all three fields together inside one narrow ManifestFormatError catch' — correctly places the cross-field check at the combined site, but the individual parser specs do not encode this allocation, leaving it implicit and easy to misplace.

Codebase: existing manifest parsers (manifest_deps, manifest_regver, manifest_disabled_builtins) each independently parse their own prefix lines with no cross-field awareness — the new parsers would naturally follow this same pattern, but the cross-field constraint breaks the pattern.
findings.finding: The plan specifies three new manifest parsers (manifest_review_phase, manifest_priority_floor, manifest_pins) that each return defaults for absent lines, but the cross-field validity rule 'a priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError' can only be enforced at a site that observes the full manifest — not by any individual parser. The plan says 'compute_plan_review_validity parses all three fields together inside one narrow ManifestFormatError catch,' correctly placing the cross-field check there. However, the individual parser specs say 'manifest_review_phase() returns planning only when the phase line is absent' (the legacy sentinel) without distinguishing the 'absent because legacy' case from the 'absent but floor-line is present' case (which should be malformed). An implementer who wires the cross-field check into an individual parser (or omits it from the combined validity-check site) will either mis-classify a malformed new-format manifest as legacy-planning or raise an error on a genuine legacy manifest that has no floor line.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins: 'manifest_review_phase() returns planning only when the phase line is absent; a priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError' and 'compute_plan_review_validity parses all three fields together'
scenarios: Implementer places the 'floor without phase' ManifestFormatError in manifest_priority_floor() so it can detect the absence of a phase line — but manifest_priority_floor() only sees its own line and cannot observe whether a phase line is absent. Result: the check is unreachable or always raises.

Implementer correctly places the check in compute_plan_review_validity but later adds a new call site (e.g., the close gate) that calls only manifest_priority_floor() in isolation — the cross-field check is silently skipped at that site.

A legacy attestation has no phase line and no floor line; manifest_review_phase() correctly returns planning. A malformed new-format attestation has a floor line and no phase line; manifest_review_phase() also returns planning. Without the combined check, the malformed attestation is silently treated as a legacy planning review, bypassing the ManifestFormatError.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'Signed plan-material pins' section: 'The cross-field constraint (priority-floor without review-phase = ManifestFormatError) is checked only at compute_plan_review_validity, which calls all three parsers in a single try/except; individual parsers remain context-free and return their per-line defaults without observing sibling lines.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact sequencing of the prerequisite evaluation sub-pass within the existing pass pipeline (e.g., 'prerequisite evaluations run after the main Pass 1 chunk pool completes but before Pass 2 verification, with their outputs merged into the shared findings index'), so implementers can wire the isolated criterion correctly without re-deriving the integration point.
evidence: Plan text: 'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist. General finder and verifier calls never receive prerequisite material.'
Plan text: 'Pass 2 receives the isolated ID map and sets yes only when the indexed original finding names exactly its in-bin canonical ID.'

Codebase: pass1.py runs criteria chunks concurrently via ThreadPoolExecutor; orchestrator/workflow_ops assembles det_blocks + Pass1 findings into the verdict; Pass 2 (pass2_completion, verify) consumes the indexed finding list. The prerequisite findings must be in the indexed list before Pass 2 runs.

ABSENCE: the plan does not name where in workflow_ops or the orchestrator the prerequisite sub-pass is inserted relative to the existing plan_review_precheck → plan_review_assemble_criteria → pass1 → pass2 → plan_review_decide pipeline.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the prerequisite consistency criterion 'runs in separate calls with isolated prompts' and 'Pass 1 returns exactly one PrerequisiteCoverageRecord per canonical prerequisite,' but does not specify at what stage in the existing three-pass review pipeline the prerequisite evaluations execute — before Pass 1 criteria chunks, concurrently with them, or as a distinct sub-phase after them. The plan also routes prerequisite findings through Pass 2 verification (adding prerequisite_attribution_valid), which requires the prerequisite findings to be present and indexed BEFORE Pass 2 runs. If prerequisite evaluations run concurrently with Pass 1 chunks (in the existing ThreadPoolExecutor), their outputs must be merged into the findings list before the Pass 2 verification loop; if they run after Pass 1, the integration point differs. The absence of a specified sequencing/integration point means the implementer must infer where in the orchestrator/workflow_ops pipeline to graft the new sub-pass.
findings.location: Settled design / Prerequisite consistency review: 'The specialized criterion runs in separate calls with isolated prompts' and 'Pass 2 receives the isolated ID map'
scenarios: Implementer grafts prerequisite evaluation as a new concurrent pool entry in pass1(), merged before Pass 2 verification — correct integration but may inadvertently expose prerequisite material to general finder criteria if isolation is not carefully enforced.

Implementer grafts it as a new workflow step between plan_review_assemble_criteria and the main pass1 call — isolated correctly but findings must then be injected into the pass1 output before Pass 2 indexing.
Implementer runs it after Pass 2 verification — too late for attribution_valid to be set on prerequisite findings, breaking the prerequisite_attribution_valid contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assert (and add a pre-rollback guard/test) that the PRECEDING deployed code version already passes unknown compiled-state keys through compaction unchanged — or document the controlled re-bootstrap consequence and add a post-rollback integrity check that detects and surfaces unexpected phase resets.
evidence: "process_snapshot restores unknown compiled-state keys and compaction drops only its explicit strip set, so an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction rather than strip it"
"Compatibility tests run old reduction plus re-compaction against a phase-bearing snapshot, then roll forward and prove the exact phase value survives"
The plan characterises this as a test obligation but does not assert that the CURRENT preceding codebase already satisfies the pass-through requirement — it is a forward constraint on the old code.
findings.finding: The rollback schema state for `plan_review_phase` in compiled-state snapshots rests on an unproven assertion about the PRECEDING code version's behavior: the plan states 'process_snapshot restores unknown compiled-state keys and compaction drops only its explicit strip set, so an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction.' If the preceding code version does NOT already pass unknown compiled-state keys through (e.g., it strips them during compaction), rolling back leaves every subsequently compacted snapshot without `plan_review_phase`. On re-upgrade those snapshots would need to re-bootstrap from compiled status — which is itself described as a fallback, but an uncontrolled silent re-bootstrap is a schema-state hazard that the plan does not explicitly bound or detect.
findings.location: Settled design → Enforcement and rollout — rollback section; also 'Pre-feature snapshots bootstrap' paragraph
scenarios: Operator rolls back to preceding code version; that version's compaction strips unknown compiled-state keys; every subsequent compaction silently drops plan_review_phase; on re-upgrade all snapshots fall back to bootstrap, potentially mis-classifying in-flight in_progress tickets as open/planning.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the rollback section: 'The preceding code version is known to pass unknown compiled-state keys through without stripping (verified by the compatibility test suite); if this cannot be confirmed for a given deployment, operators must treat plan_review_phase re-bootstrapping as a known-safe fallback and confirm no in-progress tickets received an incorrect planning phase after rollback.' Additionally, extend the compatibility test to assert the old-code compaction round-trip explicitly rather than only the forward-upgrade path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and register a named structured output contract (e.g. `plan_review_prerequisite_consistency`) for the prerequisite consistency criterion, co-located with the `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` schema and its `reason_code` mapping rules, mirroring the existing `register_contract("plan_review_findings", ...)` pattern.
evidence: Plan: 'Pass 1 returns exactly one PrerequisiteCoverageRecord per canonical prerequisite. Its schema is {prerequisite_id: canonical string, disposition: consistent|finding|indeterminate, findings: list[Finding], reason_code?: evaluation-error|output-invalid|attribution-invalid, detail?: string}'
Plan: 'The specialized criterion runs in separate calls with isolated prompts.'
No `plan_review_prerequisite_consistency` contract exists in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py or src/rebar/schemas/
All existing sub-calls register a named contract: plan_review_findings, plan_review_verification, plan_review_novelty, plan_review_completion, plan_review_coach
findings.finding: The new prerequisite consistency criterion has no specified structured output schema (no pydantic model, no JSON schema contract registered via `register_contract`). The plan names `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` with a JSON schema ({prerequisite_id, disposition, findings, reason_code?, detail?}) and maps model/provider failure → evaluation-error, parse-invalid → output-invalid, attribution-invalid → attribution-invalid, but these are described in narrative paragraphs far from each other and no corresponding LLM-callable structured output contract is defined. By comparison, every existing criterion uses a registered contract (e.g. `plan_review_findings`, `plan_review_verification`). An executor building this feature has no basis for defining the prompt output shape.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review (Pass 1 returns exactly one PrerequisiteCoverageRecord per canonical prerequisite)
scenarios: Executor implements prerequisite calls without a registered contract, producing inconsistent output shapes that silently degrade to indeterminate.
The reason_code mapping rules (model failure → evaluation-error) are in a different paragraph from the schema, leading to mismatched implementations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly how `prerequisite_attribution_valid` is added to Pass 2: name the extension mechanism (plan-review-specific verification schema override vs. separate sub-call), define the 'isolated ID map' as a concrete prompt input field/structure, and confirm it does not alter the shared kernel `plan_review_verification` contract used by code-review.
evidence: Plan: 'Pass 2 receives the isolated ID map and sets yes only when the indexed original finding names exactly its in-bin canonical ID.'
Plan: 'The shared index-keyed Pass 2 verification schema gains optional prerequisite_attribution_valid = yes|no|na; non-prerequisite findings use na.'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:129-134: '_pass2_model = plan_review_verification_model' — the plan_review_verification contract is owned by the kernel and shared with code-review
No definition of 'isolated ID map' as a prompt input field or schema field exists in the plan
findings.finding: `prerequisite_attribution_valid = yes|no|na` is added to the existing Pass 2 verification schema, but the plan never specifies how the 'isolated ID map' is supplied to Pass 2 — whether as a new prompt field, an extension to the existing `plan_review_verification` contract, a separate sub-call, or an ambient context variable. The decision rule ('sets yes only when the indexed original finding names exactly its in-bin canonical ID') is stated without a co-located schema extension spec. The existing `plan_review_verification` contract is a fixed shape owned by the kernel; extending it silently breaks backward compatibility with code-review which shares the same kernel.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review ('The shared index-keyed Pass 2 verification schema gains optional prerequisite_attribution_valid = yes|no|na')
scenarios: Executor extends the shared kernel verification schema, breaking code-review's Pass 2 contract.
Executor creates a plan-review-specific verification schema extension but has no spec for what 'isolated ID map' contains or how it's serialized into the prompt.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a consolidated `pin_status` classification decision table co-located with the `TargetPinDetail` schema definition, mapping each observable condition (fingerprint match, fingerprint mismatch, target deleted/missing, parse error, no pins present) to exactly one `pin_status` enum value.
evidence: Plan (Signed plan-material pins): 'A pinned target that has been deleted or is missing makes the pin stale and requires relationship repair followed by re-review' → implies stale-pin-missing
Plan (Enforcement and rollout): 'pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned'
Plan (Signed plan-material pins): 'At later validity checks, deletion of a previously pinned target is missing-target drift'
These three rules are spread across two separate subsections, never assembled into one decision table alongside the TargetPinDetail schema
findings.finding: The `pin_status` computation rule for each `TargetPinDetail` — specifically, how `stale-pin-drift`, `stale-pin-missing`, and `malformed-pin` are distinguished during validity evaluation — is stated in the enforcement/rollout section, separated from the `TargetPinDetail` schema definition and from `compute_plan_review_validity`. There is no co-location of the decision rule ('deletion of a previously pinned target is missing-target drift' → `stale-pin-missing`; fingerprint mismatch → `stale-pin-drift`; parse error → `malformed-pin`) with the schema's `pin_status` vocabulary. This violates the T8 co-location criterion: a processing protocol is separated from the schema that needs it.
findings.location: Settled design → Enforcement and rollout (compute_plan_review_validity contract) and Signed plan-material pins (resolvability rules)
scenarios: Executor implementing compute_plan_review_validity must chase three different plan sections to assemble the complete pin_status decision table, introducing risk of missing the 'canonical-ID mismatch counts as unresolvable' edge case.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Pin status classification' subsection adjacent to the TargetPinDetail definition: current (fingerprints match), stale-pin-drift (fingerprint mismatch), stale-pin-missing (target deleted/missing at validity-check time), malformed-pin (parse error on the stored pin line), legacy-unpinned (no pin lines present in the attestation).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Formalize the `plan-material-pin` line grammar: specify the exact token count (exactly 4 space-delimited tokens after the `plan-material-pin:` prefix: role, canonical-id, fingerprint), allowed character sets for each token, and how each violation maps to the `ManifestFormatError` category (missing-token vs. extra-token vs. malformed).
evidence: Plan: 'plan-material-pin syntax is plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>'
Plan: 'New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines'
No definition of token count, delimiter (space vs. any whitespace), fingerprint character set, or maximum length is provided
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a `priority-floor` manifest line without a `review-phase` line raises `ManifestFormatError`, and that `manifest_pins()` raises `ManifestFormatError` on 'missing-token, extra-token' pin lines — but the grammar of a valid `plan-material-pin` line (number of tokens, delimiter, allowed characters in the fingerprint field) is never formally stated. Only the example syntax `plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>` is given. It is unspecified whether whitespace inside `<canonical-ticket-id>` or `<material-fingerprint>` is a 'malformed' or 'extra-token' error, and how many tokens constitute 'extra-token' vs. a valid line with trailing content.
findings.location: Settled design → Signed plan-material pins ('plan-material-pin syntax is plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>')
scenarios: Two implementers split the pin line differently (splitting on all whitespace vs. exactly one space), yielding different 'extra-token' verdicts for fingerprints containing unusual characters.
A canonical ID with a hyphen-separated UUID format is inadvertently parsed as two tokens if the wrong delimiter is used.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A valid pin line is `plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <hex-fingerprint>` where exactly three space-delimited tokens follow the colon-space prefix; fewer tokens raise missing-token, more tokens raise extra-token, a non-hex fingerprint raises malformed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicit delivery ordering: acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c and 5967-4476-7f9f-442d must be complete before 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 can be integrated, and document the PlanRelationSnapshot and threshold function as exported cross-child interfaces.
evidence: 7c27 Implementation: 'Consume the pin story's PlanRelationSnapshot contract'
7c27 Implementation: 'Reuse the phase story's threshold function; do not add a separate decide pipeline'
Neither 7c27 nor acaf nor 5967 state an explicit delivery ordering requirement between these children.
findings.finding: 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 (prerequisite criterion) consumes the PlanRelationSnapshot contract defined in acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c and the threshold function defined in 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, but neither child states an explicit implementation-ordering dependency on those foundational children. If 7c27 is delivered before acaf or 5967, the consumed interfaces do not yet exist.
findings.location: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56
scenarios: Parallel-track delivery assigns 7c27 to a different team before acaf is complete, leaving PlanRelationSnapshot undefined.
7c27 is implemented first and adds a placeholder for PlanRelationSnapshot that diverges from acaf's final definition, requiring later reconciliation.
7c27's threshold reuse wires to an internal symbol in 5967 that was later refactored, causing a silent interface mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 7c27's implementation section: 'Depends on acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c for PlanRelationSnapshot and on 5967-4476-7f9f-442d for the shared Pass-3 threshold function; both must be available before integration.' Add an exported interface contract to acaf for PlanRelationSnapshot and to 5967 for the threshold helper.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign explicit ownership of a combined build_manifest integration test to one child (acaf or 5967) that validates the full canonical ordering from the parent's settled design with both phase/floor and pin lines present simultaneously.
evidence: 5967 Implementation: 'In build_manifest final order, insert review-phase immediately after advisory, then optional priority-floor, before rebar-version'
acaf Implementation: 'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear after optional verified-at-sha and before dep lines'

Parent settled design specifies the exact combined order: ...advisory, review-phase, optional priority-floor, rebar-version, optional regver, optional disabled_builtins, optional refreshed-from, optional verified-at-sha, zero or more plan-material-pin lines, then zero or more dep lines.
Neither child says 'this child also integrates the other child's build_manifest changes' or 'owned by X, consumed by Y'.
findings.finding: Both 5967-4476-7f9f-442d and acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c modify the build_manifest function to insert new lines in a precise canonical order, but neither child claims ownership of coordinating the two additions into the single correct combined manifest ordering, creating a dependency gap that could produce an invalid manifest if the two insertions are merged independently.
findings.location: child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c
scenarios: 5967 adds phase/floor lines to build_manifest at the correct position; acaf adds pin lines at the correct position; when merged, the combined order is correct only if both are rebased correctly — with no stated coordination owner, integration merge errors could silently produce an incorrect manifest ordering.
Deterministic manifest ordering tests in each child pass independently (each tests its own lines) but the combined manifest test is missing, allowing an ordering regression at integration.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c's verification: 'Integration test verifies the full combined manifest ordering from the parent design canonical order, with review-phase, priority-floor, rebar-version, and plan-material-pin lines all present, using a fixture that simulates both additions simultaneously.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the foundational-to-consuming delivery dependency chain: acaf+5967 must be integrated before 75de; 75de must be integrated before 2bbd, cb6b; all must be integrated before 145e.
evidence: 75de Implementation: 'Compare signed pins against current narrow material' — requires pins from acaf
75de Implementation: phase_status in DerivedPlanReviewHealth — requires phase parsers from 5967
2bbd Implementation: 'Call the validity story's compute_plan_review_validity close profile' — requires 75de which requires acaf+5967
cb6b Implementation: 'Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' — requires 75de
145e Implementation references: 'legacy unpinned attestations; emission while enforcement is off' — all downstream of acaf+5967+75de
No child's AC or implementation section lists 'depends on child X' in a way that constrains delivery ordering.
findings.finding: The consuming children 75de-02b0-d454-410b, 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00, cb6b-24b4-1056-4ee6, and 145e-52a9-26e3-4209 each depend on foundational deliverables from acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c (pins, PlanRelationSnapshot) and 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (phase projection, parsers, pre-sign transaction), but no child states these cross-child ordering dependencies, creating a structural delivery risk when the epic is executed.
scenarios: Team implements 75de first to provide the validity contract, but pins don't exist yet (acaf not done), so DerivedPlanReviewHealth.targets is always empty — the derived health contract appears to work but is silently incomplete.
145e lifecycle tests run against stubs for acaf/5967 functionality that diverge from final behavior, giving false confidence in the end-to-end proof.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly designate the max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) threshold resolution as an exported shared function in 5967-4476-7f9f-442d's design, and add an AC confirming 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 imports rather than re-implements it.
evidence: 7c27 Implementation: 'Reuse the phase story's threshold function; do not add a separate decide pipeline'

5967 Implementation describes threshold only as part of Pass-3 blocking-threshold resolution: 'effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) for blocking criteria' — no statement that this is exported or shared.
5967 ACs do not include an AC for 'the threshold function is available as a shared interface for other children'.
findings.finding: 5967-4476-7f9f-442d's implementation and ACs do not explicitly designate the Pass-3 threshold helper as a reusable exported function, yet 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 requires it ('Reuse the phase story's threshold function'). If 5967 implements it as a private/internal helper, 7c27 cannot reuse it without either duplicating it or requesting a design change.
scenarios: 5967 ships the threshold logic as a module-private function; 7c27 implementer duplicates it, introducing the risk of divergence if the 0.80 floor policy changes.
7c27 imports a symbol that 5967 later refactors into a different module, causing an import error caught only at integration time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 5967's implementation: 'Export the effective_block_threshold resolution as a shared utility function usable by other review components including the prerequisite criterion story.' Add a corresponding AC: 'The threshold resolution helper is exposed as a shared import boundary; no duplicate implementation exists.'

findings.finding: The parent design specifies 'the completion-verifier branch does not parse plan-review-only phase, floor, or pins' as a deliberate isolation constraint. No child's acceptance criteria or test plan owns verification that the completion-verifier branch is NOT modified to call manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins(). This is a named consumer of the manifest-parsing infrastructure whose isolation invariant is uncovered.
findings.location: No child — gap is absent from the full sibling roster
findings.suggested_fix: In 75de or 5967, add an acceptance criterion: 'The completion-verifier branch does not call manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins(); a negative-path unit test confirms no ManifestFormatError is raised on a completion attestation containing unexpected phase/floor/pin lines.'

findings.finding: The `indeterminate` coverage record's `reason_code` enum (evaluation-error, output-invalid, attribution-invalid) is defined inline in the prerequisite-consistency prose but is never co-located with the Pass 1 output schema. No schema definition names these values as a closed enum, so implementors of Pass 2/Pass 3 that branch on `reason_code` have no canonical reference.
findings.location: Prerequisite consistency review section: 'indeterminate requires reason_code exactly evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid plus optional diagnostic detail'

findings.finding: The plan says manifest_review_phase() returns 'planning' when 'the phase line is absent,' and separately that a planning-mode review is signed with review-phase: planning (no special case for omission). However the processing rule for a manifest that contains a priority-floor: line but no review-phase: line is unspecified — an implementor must guess whether this is malformed, treated as execution with planning mode, or treated as planning.
findings.location: Signed plan-material pins section: 'manifest_review_phase() returns planning only when the phase line is absent'; Execution reviews section: 'Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: a manifest carrying priority-floor: without review-phase: is treated as malformed (ManifestFormatError) since the floor is meaningless without a phase context, consistent with the 'missing, nonnumeric, or range-invalid execution floors are malformed' rule already established for execution attestations.

findings.finding: The DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract lists 'canonical target details' as a field but never enumerates the sub-fields (e.g., canonical_id, role, pinned_fingerprint, current_fingerprint). An executor implementing the health output for five named surfaces — detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, web ticket-review UI — cannot determine what to render without knowing the exact field set.
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout — 'compute_plan_review_validity … adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth. That nested contract has pin_status … enforced boolean; and canonical target details.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'canonical target details' with an explicit sub-schema, e.g.: 'canonical target details: a list of per-pin records each containing {canonical_id: string, role: child|prerequisite, pinned_fingerprint: string, current_fingerprint: string|null, pin_status: current|stale-pin-drift|stale-pin-missing|malformed-pin|legacy-unpinned}.'

findings.finding: DerivedPlanReviewHealth 'canonical target details' sub-schema is referenced but never defined. The plan names this field as part of the nested health contract but never enumerates its sub-fields (e.g., canonical_id, role, pinned_fingerprint, current_fingerprint, current_material).
findings.location: Enforcement and rollout section: 'compute_plan_review_validity preserves its top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract and adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth. That nested contract has pin_status ... and canonical target details.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `TargetPinDetail` sub-schema inline in the 'Enforcement and rollout' section: `{canonical_id: str, role: 'child'|'prerequisite', pinned_fingerprint: str, current_fingerprint: str|None, drift_kind: pin_status_value}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement step (e.g., replay a sample of past reviews under the 0.80 floor and record how many findings shift from advisory to blocking) to validate the 0.80 threshold before it becomes a signed protocol value, or document an explicit retrospective cadence to revisit it after initial rollout.
evidence: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
No spike, measurement step, pilot, or retrospective review cadence is mentioned for this threshold.
'A runtime floor flag is intentionally not added' — so there is no easy way to observe the system at different floor values.
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-review priority-floor is an asserted policy value with no measurement plan, spike, or pilot to validate that it is appropriate in practice — the plan explicitly disavows empirical grounding ('not an empirically claimed optimum') but provides no path to learn whether it produces too many spurious blocks or too few caught risks across real reviews.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate: '0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config'
scenarios: The floor is too high: many execution-phase reviews are blocked on minor findings that would not have been blocking under the existing threshold, creating friction and eroding trust in the gate.
The floor is too low: the gate fails to catch the material risks it was intended to address, providing false confidence.
A future policy change requires manifest migration discussion that could have been avoided had the initial value been empirically validated.
findings.suggested_fix: Before finalizing 0.80 as the signed protocol floor, replay 20–30 historical execution-phase review sessions against the threshold and record the false-block rate (findings that were advisory under project thresholds but blocking under 0.80) and miss rate (findings that would have been blocking under 0.80 but were not caught). Use these to confirm or adjust the floor. Document the chosen value and its empirical basis in the design notes.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
